title: Compression Impact Guide
summary: Understand how quality preset, motion level, keyframe interval, hours, retention, and camera count affect storage savings and image-quality risk.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates how compression choices change retained storage demand and how much quality risk those settings may introduce.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when reducing bitrate or storage footprint is being considered and the design needs to keep image usefulness in view.
bullets: Early video-storage design planning.
Expansion review or recorder replacement.
Existing-system cleanup, validation, or troubleshooting.
Explaining storage, retention, and quality tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on this tool and explain what each value represents in practical video-storage planning terms.

items.label: Baseline Bitrate (Mbps)
items.description: The starting bitrate before the compression change is applied.
items.whyItMatters: Baseline bitrate defines how much storage is available to save or risk reducing.
items.commonMistake: Starting from a guessed bitrate instead of measured or estimated stream behavior.

links.label: Estimate the baseline bitrate
links.note: Use this when the baseline profile is not known.

items.label: Quality Preset
items.whyItMatters: Quality preset controls the tradeoff between file size and visible detail.
items.commonMistake: Choosing the smallest file size without checking whether faces, plates, or scene detail remain usable.

items.label: Motion Level
items.description: The amount of motion or scene change expected in the video.
items.whyItMatters: Motion affects how hard compression has to work and how visible artifacts may become.
items.commonMistake: Testing compression on calm scenes and deploying to busy scenes.

items.label: Keyframe Interval (seconds)
items.description: The time between full reference frames in the video stream.
items.whyItMatters: Longer intervals can improve compression but may affect seeking, recovery, or quality during change.
items.commonMistake: Extending keyframe interval for storage savings without checking VMS playback and event review behavior.

items.label: Hours per Day
items.description: The number of recorded hours per day affected by the compression setting.
items.whyItMatters: Recorded hours convert bitrate reduction into daily storage impact.
items.commonMistake: Assuming a compression change is minor when it applies to continuous recording.

items.label: Days of Retention
items.description: The retention period used to calculate total storage impact.
items.whyItMatters: Retention length multiplies compression savings or quality risk over time.
items.commonMistake: Only checking daily savings when the system retains weeks of footage.

items.label: Camera Count
items.description: The number of cameras using the compression profile.
items.whyItMatters: Camera count scales storage savings and risk across the system.
items.commonMistake: Applying a risky preset broadly instead of limiting it to suitable scene types.

links.label: Compare codec effect too
links.note: Use this when codec and compression changes are both under review.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means compression appears reasonable for the assumed storage and quality balance. Watch means quality, motion, or keyframe assumptions deserve review. Risk means the compression plan may save storage at the cost of usable footage.
bullets: Healthy: the design appears manageable based on the entered assumptions.
Watch: the design may still work, but the assumptions deserve review.
Risk: add capacity, reduce demand, adjust policy, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most video-storage planning problems come from treating bitrate, storage, retention, RAID, and failure behavior as separate decisions even though they shape the same final retention promise.
bullets: Sizing storage from one average bitrate without checking peak, motion, codec, and retention behavior.
Treating nominal capacity as usable capacity after RAID, filesystem, spare, and operational reserves.
Ignoring growth, camera additions, quality changes, and retention policy changes after day one.
Failing to document assumptions before the recorder, storage tier, or archive policy is selected.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, VMS platform limits, camera-specific behavior, storage vendor guidance, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Camera bitrate can vary significantly with scene motion, lighting, compression settings, and codec behavior.
Usable storage should account for redundancy, reserved space, filesystem overhead, and operational margin.
Retention targets should be validated against stakeholder expectations, evidence needs, and system operation.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, expand, and troubleshoot.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal design review, manufacturer validation, field testing, or project-specific responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual VMS, recorder, camera model, codec profile, storage platform, and network path being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations for retention, archive access, failure behavior, and acceptable image quality.
Document assumptions before implementation so future changes can be evaluated against the original design intent.
